info.text: In this demo we'll use the netnet-standard-library along with the Tone.js library to load and play an audio file. Of course, you can do all of this with the browser's native APIs, JavaScript libraries are just abstractions that reduce boilerplate and let us express our ideas with less code.
info.title: getting started
import libs
info.text: As with anytime we're using libraries, we'll first need to import them using script tags, then in another script tag we'll write our code.

info.title: Gain Node
info.text: Here we'll use the Tone.js library to create a "gain node", essentialy a volume control, and set it to 75%. We'll use .toDestination() to connect it to your computer audio's output settings, likely your speaker or headphones.

info.title: Audio Player
info.text: This loads the audio file, by orangefreesounds, into a Tone.js "player" and connect it to the gain node so the volume setting applies.

info.title: play function
info.text: We wrap the play logic in a function so we can call it later from a click. It's async because we'll wait for Tone.js to be ready and for the file to finish loading before triggering the play logic.

info.title: unlock audio
info.text: Browsers block audio until the user interacts with the page. This "unlocks" audio the first time you click.

info.title: wait for file
info.text: This waits until Tone.js has finished fetching and decoding the audio file so playback starts cleanly.

info.title: play sound
info.text: We stop first so the sound always restarts from the beginning each time you click.

info.title: create button
info.text: We create a button element with the nn.min.js library, set its text content to "play," and add it to the body so it shows up on the page.

info.title: position button
info.text: By default, positions are measured from the top-left of the button. Switching the origin to the center makes centering math easier. We place the button at half the width and half the height of the window, which puts it in the center of the screen.

info.title: play on click
info.text: Modern browsers require a user action to start audio, which is why I'm calling playSound() from a click event.

info.title: other ways
info.text: As I mentioned earlier, we could do all of this without libraries, but not only would it require more code, if we wanted to use any of the other methods provided by these libraries we'd have to write all those from scratch as well.
